figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of static and dynamic (e.g., FAMA) agentic orchestration.

Table 1: Comparison of different open-source agents on τ -bench across five runs. Note that Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct is used as the model backbone of the sub-agents in the IRMA and FAMA agentic frameworks.
Figure 3: Comparing τ -bench performance across multiple LLMs being tested as user agents.

Table 2: Token and latency cost analysis of various methods for τ -Airline and τ -Retail. The tokens reported in Columns 2–5 refer to the sum of (i) tokens generated by the main tool-use assistant (Column 6) and (ii) additional overhead tokens introduced by helper agents (Column 7). “Assistant tokens” denote the average tokens produced solely by the primary tool-calling agent, while “overhead tokens” correspond to the average tokens generated by helper agents in the IRMA and FAMA settings. ‘ReAct-non-thinking’ and ‘ReAct-thinking’ represent non-thinking and thinking models used for ReAct method.
Figure 10: Comparison of agent performance in τ -bench across different values of k, where k denotes the number of most recent user queries retained.
Figure 12: Statistical comparison of error categories across open-source LLMs in τ -trait environment, as identified by the Orchestrator Agent.
Figure 14: Statistical comparison of error categories across open-source LLMs in ACEBench, as identified by the Orchestrator Agent.
Figure 13: Statistics of recommendations produced by the Mitigation Agent across different open source agents.

Figure 2: Overview of the FAMA framework. Stage 1: A baseline agent, without multi-agent assistance, is executed on domain tasks. Stage 2: failed tasks are analyzed and categorized by a human or an agentic framework. Stage 2.1: Independent agents focus on distinct issues and analyze the causes of failure through task-specific conversations. Stage 2.2: An orchestrator agent identifies the primary reasons for failure. Stage 2.3: A mitigation agent selects the most appropriate helper agents to address the identified issues. Stage 3: the baseline agent is re-executed using only the helper agents recommended by the mitigation agent.
Figure 4: Statistical comparison of error categories across open-source LLMs in τ -retail, as identified by the Orchestrator Agent.
Figure 15: Statistics of recommendations produced by the Mitigation Agent across different open source agents for ACEBench.
Figure 8: Comparison of tool calling agents that rely on different agentic combinations.

Table 4: Comparison of different open-source agents on τ -trait across five runs. Note that Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct is used as the model backbone of the sub-agents of IRMA and FAMA agentic frameworks.
Figure 5: Statistics of recommendations produced by the Mitigation Agent across open source agents for τ -bench.
Figure 6: Performance comparison of Qwen3-14B and Qwen3-32B (thinking variants) across methods on τ -bench.

Figure 7: Token overhead (%) and latency (seconds) across different methods with Qwen3-32B. See Table 2 for detailed statistics. ‘ReAct-nt’ and ‘ReAct-t’ represent non-thinking and thinking models used for ReAct method.
Table 5: The different agents used in the FAMA framework for τ -bench.
